question.text: A bull spread in commodity futures is created when:

answer.text: The actual spread is more than the fundamental spread, with the expectation that it will narrow
The actual spread is less than fundamental, expecting widening
The trader expects prices to rise sharply
Both legs are bought at the same time

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Bull spread: buy near-month + sell far-month when actual spread > fundamental average. Expectation: the difference narrows. Profit comes from spread narrowing. **The Trap:** Bear spread is when actual < fundamental and expected to widen. A bull spread is about the spread relationship, not directional price view. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series VIII covers bull and bear spreads in equity options. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** 'Bull spread' in futures context means the near-month is undervalued — buying near and selling far profits when the near-month catches up.
